summary: Spolsky reviews two Macintosh library management applications, Delicious Library and Booxter, for cataloging a physical book collection. While Delicious Library has charming visual design and easy barcode scanning, it lacks Library of Congress integration, which Spolsky considers essential for organizing books by topic. Booxter provides this LOC integration but neither app can print spine labels, creating a workflow gap. The post illustrates how a visually appealing product can lose to a plainer competitor that solves the actual problem.
key_points: Delicious Library wins on visual design but lacks Library of Congress catalog number import, making it insufficient for proper book organization
Library of Congress classification is valuable because it groups books by topic, enabling browsable shelves rather than arbitrary ordering
Booxter has a simpler UI but solves the core problem by connecting to the Library of Congress
Neither app supports printing spine labels at scan time, which would be the natural workflow for actually shelving books correctly
money_quotes: Delicious Library does not have any way to import data from the Library of Congress.
When you do this, books on similar topics tend to wind up near each other on the shelves.
Somebody has gone to a lot of trouble choosing a card catalog number that put similar books next to each other.

I've always found that open-shelved libraries with books in order according to some reasonable card catalog system are far superior to libraries with books in order according to acquisition date, size, color, IQ, or IDENTITY column.
Printing a spine label at scan time would make for a vastly easier workflow.
key_insight: A visually delightful application that misses the user's core workflow need will lose to a plainer one that solves the actual problem.
